abstract: This feature article compares microplastics (MPs) and engineered nanoparticles (ENPs) as particulate contaminants in aquatic environments to identify transferrable knowledge from ENP exposure assessment to advance MP research. The paper addresses four key research domains: (1) formation, sources and emission pathways; (2) environmental transformation and transport processes; (3) analytical methods for characterization and monitoring; and (4) representative laboratory experiment design. While both MPs and ENPs share particulate nature requiring kinetic rather than thermodynamic approaches, they differ significantly in size ranges, densities, and aging mechanisms. MPs typically range from micrometers to millimeters with low densities near water, whereas ENPs are <100 nm with higher densities. The paper highlights that secondary MPs (from plastic fragmentation) dominate over primary MPs, and discusses challenges in standardizing analytical methods, concentration metrics, and laboratory protocols for studying MP behavior and fate in aquatic systems.

abstract: Microplastics (MPs) have been identified as contaminants of emerging concern in aquatic environments
comparing ENP and MPs based on their similarities as particulate contaminants, whereas critically discussing specific differences

MPs are only rarely deliberately incorporated into a product to serve a specific function, but generally occur as so-called secondary MPs, resulting from the unintentional release and fragmentation of larger pieces of plastics

significant gaps remain in our understanding of several crucial aspects of MP exposure and risk assessment, including the quantification of emissions, dominant fate processes, types of analytical tools required for characterization and monitoring, and adequate laboratory protocols
